cards.title: Manipulative Therapy — Principles & Classification

alerts.label: Definition
alerts.text: Manipulative therapy encompasses a spectrum of hands-on techniques applied to joints, muscles, fascia, and soft tissues to restore normal biomechanical function, reduce pain, and promote healing. In BNYS practice it includes spinal manipulation, mobilisation, and soft tissue techniques that complement naturopathic care.

rows: Thrust technique — rapid small-amplitude impulse at end of joint range
Acute LBP, facet joint syndrome, cervicogenic headache, rib dysfunction
Sustained or rhythmic pressure within joint range
Joint stiffness, subacute pain, hypomobility without acute inflammation
Patient's voluntary isometric contraction against therapist resistance, followed by passive stretch
Sacroiliac dysfunction, tight hip flexors, cervical restriction
Sustained low-load mechanical pressure into fascial restrictions
Fibromyalgia, postural dysfunction, chronic soft tissue pain
Passive positioning of joint in position of ease for 90 seconds
Acute muscle guarding, tender points, somatic dysfunction
Sustained or intermittent longitudinal distraction of spinal segments

pearls.label: 🟡 Spinal Manipulation — Audible Cavitation
pearls.text: The "crack" heard during HVLA manipulation is cavitation — rapid formation and collapse of gas bubbles (CO₂, N₂) in synovial fluid when intra-articular pressure drops suddenly. It does NOT indicate therapeutic success — benefit comes from neurological effects: reduced α-motor neuron excitability, altered pain gating at dorsal horn, and proprioceptive reset. Some patients respond without any audible release. NEVER force audible cavitation.

cards._legacyHtml:
Definition
Manipulative therapy encompasses a spectrum of hands-on techniques applied to joints, muscles, fascia, and soft tissues to restore normal biomechanical function, reduce pain, and promote healing. In BNYS practice it includes spinal manipulation, mobilisation, and soft tissue techniques that complement naturopathic care.
TypeTechniqueTarget TissueIndication
High-Velocity Low-Amplitude (HVLA)Thrust technique — rapid small-amplitude impulse at end of joint rangeZygapophyseal joints, costovertebral jointsAcute LBP, facet joint syndrome, cervicogenic headache, rib dysfunction
Low-Velocity Low-Amplitude (LVLA)Sustained or rhythmic pressure within joint rangePeripheral joints, spinal facetsJoint stiffness, subacute pain, hypomobility without acute inflammation
Muscle Energy Technique (MET)Patient's voluntary isometric contraction against therapist resistance, followed by passive stretchMuscles, tendons, joint capsuleSacroiliac dysfunction, tight hip flexors, cervical restriction
Myofascial Release (MFR)Sustained low-load mechanical pressure into fascial restrictionsFascia, connective tissueFibromyalgia, postural dysfunction, chronic soft tissue pain
Strain-CounterstrainPassive positioning of joint in position of ease for 90 secondsTender points, muscle spindlesAcute muscle guarding, tender points, somatic dysfunction
TractionSustained or intermittent longitudinal distraction of spinal segmentsIntervertebral discs, nerve rootsDisc herniation, radiculopathy, cervical spondylosis
🟡 Spinal Manipulation — Audible Cavitation
The "crack" heard during HVLA manipulation is cavitation — rapid formation and collapse of gas bubbles (CO₂, N₂) in synovial fluid when intra-articular pressure drops suddenly. It does NOT indicate therapeutic success — benefit comes from neurological effects: reduced α-motor neuron excitability, altered pain gating at dorsal horn, and proprioceptive reset. Some patients respond without any audible release. NEVER force audible cavitation.

cards.title: Spinal Manipulation — Regional Techniques

alerts.label: Pre-Manipulation Assessment — MANDATORY
alerts.text: Screen ALL patients before manipulation: rule out red flags (fracture, tumour, infection, cord compression, AAA), assess vertebrobasilar insufficiency (VBI) for cervical manipulation, check anticoagulant status. Always obtain informed consent.

rows: Pain reduction, faster RTW vs medication alone

steps.title: Cervical Spine Assessment
steps.text: VBI screen: De Kleyn's test (sustained extension + rotation for 30 sec — stop if vertigo, nystagmus, diplopia, dysarthria, drop attack). Contraindicate cervical HVLA if positive. ALWAYS assess for atlanto-axial instability (rheumatoid, Down syndrome — sharp's angle test). Cervical arterial dissection risk is rare (1:100,000) but serious.

steps.title: Thoracic Spine
steps.text: Most amenable to HVLA — lower risk than cervical. Seated or prone techniques. Commonly used for: mid-back pain, costochondral dysfunction, respiratory conditions (rib mobilisation improves chest wall expansion), referred pain mimicking cardiac symptoms. T4 syndrome: pain + parasthesia in upper limbs from T4 dysfunction.

steps.title: Lumbar Spine
steps.text: Side-posture technique most common. Patient in side-lying, hips flexed 45°. Localise to target segment before thrust. Used for: facet joint syndrome, sacroiliac dysfunction, acute/chronic LBP. Avoid if disc herniation with neurological deficit (acute radiculopathy) — prefer mobilisation or traction instead. McKenzie assessment first for disc pathology.

steps.title: Sacroiliac Joint (SIJ)
steps.text: Assess with: FABER, FADIR, Posterior Shear, Distraction, Compression, Sacral Thrust, Gaenslen's — 3 positive tests = 91% specificity for SIJ dysfunction. MET is first choice for SIJ: patient pushes knee into table, therapist resists, followed by passive correction. Common in postpartum women and runners.

cards._legacyHtml:
Pre-Manipulation Assessment — MANDATORY
Screen ALL patients before manipulation: rule out red flags (fracture, tumour, infection, cord compression, AAA), assess vertebrobasilar insufficiency (VBI) for cervical manipulation, check anticoagulant status. Always obtain informed consent.
1
Cervical Spine Assessment VBI screen: De Kleyn's test (sustained extension + rotation for 30 sec — stop if vertigo, nystagmus, diplopia, dysarthria, drop attack). Contraindicate cervical HVLA if positive. ALWAYS assess for atlanto-axial instability (rheumatoid, Down syndrome — sharp's angle test). Cervical arterial dissection risk is rare (1:100,000) but serious.
2
Thoracic Spine Most amenable to HVLA — lower risk than cervical. Seated or prone techniques. Commonly used for: mid-back pain, costochondral dysfunction, respiratory conditions (rib mobilisation improves chest wall expansion), referred pain mimicking cardiac symptoms. T4 syndrome: pain + parasthesia in upper limbs from T4 dysfunction.
3
Lumbar Spine Side-posture technique most common. Patient in side-lying, hips flexed 45°. Localise to target segment before thrust. Used for: facet joint syndrome, sacroiliac dysfunction, acute/chronic LBP. Avoid if disc herniation with neurological deficit (acute radiculopathy) — prefer mobilisation or traction instead. McKenzie assessment first for disc pathology.
4
Sacroiliac Joint (SIJ) Assess with: FABER, FADIR, Posterior Shear, Distraction, Compression, Sacral Thrust, Gaenslen's — 3 positive tests = 91% specificity for SIJ dysfunction. MET is first choice for SIJ: patient pushes knee into table, therapist resists, followed by passive correction. Common in postpartum women and runners.
ConditionBest TechniqueEvidence LevelExpected Outcome
Acute LBP (<6 weeks)Lumbar HVLA or mobilisation★★★ Grade APain reduction, faster RTW vs medication alone
Chronic LBPManipulation + exercise★★★ Grade AEqual to analgesics, superior long-term
Neck pain (non-specific)Cervical mobilisation + MET★★★ Grade ASignificant pain and disability reduction
Cervicogenic headacheCervical manipulation + exercise★★ Grade BFrequency and intensity reduction
Shoulder (adhesive capsulitis)Grade III–IV joint mobilisation★★ Grade BROM improvement, pain reduction
Knee OALumbar + knee mobilisation★★ Grade BPain and function improvement

cards.title: Soft Tissue Manipulation — Practical Protocols

rows: Sustained ischaemic compression on trigger point (8–12 sec), followed by stretching of muscle
Myofascial pain syndrome, tension headache, fibromyalgia
Anticoagulants (heavy pressure), active infection, malignancy at site
Slow strokes with sustained pressure across muscle fibres, into fascial layers
Chronic muscle tension, postural pain, adhesions, DOMS
Acute injury, thrombophlebitis, active inflammatory arthritis, skin breakdown
Stainless steel tools glide over skin with emollient — creates therapeutic microtrauma to promote remodelling
Plantar fasciitis, Achilles tendinopathy, scar tissue, chronic tendinosis
Open wounds, DVT, bone malignancy at site, skin fragility
Static or dynamic negative pressure cups on skin — decompresses soft tissue (opposite to massage compression)
LBP, myofascial pain, respiratory conditions, ITB syndrome
Bleeding disorders, varicose veins at site, active skin inflammation
Acupuncture needles into trigger points — elicit local twitch response; different from acupuncture (anatomical targets, not meridian-based)
Trigger point pain, post-stroke spasticity, DOMS
Needle phobia, anticoagulants (relative), pregnancy (certain regions), pacemaker (e-stim added)

pearls.label: 🟡 Manipulative Therapy in BNYS Practice
pearls.text: BNYS practitioners integrate manipulative approaches within naturopathic frameworks. Key principle: manipulation addresses structural obstructions to the body's self-healing force (Vis Medicatrix Naturae). Always combine with: hydrotherapy (contrast baths pre-treatment to warm tissues), nutritional anti-inflammatories (turmeric, omega-3), yoga-based stretching post-treatment, and patient education on posture/ergonomics. Documentation: record technique, segment treated, post-treatment response, and patient consent.

cards._legacyHtml:
TechniqueMethodDurationIndicationContraindication
Trigger Point TherapySustained ischaemic compression on trigger point (8–12 sec), followed by stretching of muscle60–90 sec per point, 3–4 points/sessionMyofascial pain syndrome, tension headache, fibromyalgiaAnticoagulants (heavy pressure), active infection, malignancy at site
Deep Tissue MassageSlow strokes with sustained pressure across muscle fibres, into fascial layers30–60 min sessionChronic muscle tension, postural pain, adhesions, DOMSAcute injury, thrombophlebitis, active inflammatory arthritis, skin breakdown
Instrument-Assisted Soft Tissue Mobilisation (IASTM)Stainless steel tools glide over skin with emollient — creates therapeutic microtrauma to promote remodelling30–60 sec per area, 2–3x/weekPlantar fasciitis, Achilles tendinopathy, scar tissue, chronic tendinosisOpen wounds, DVT, bone malignancy at site, skin fragility
Cupping TherapyStatic or dynamic negative pressure cups on skin — decompresses soft tissue (opposite to massage compression)5–15 min per areaLBP, myofascial pain, respiratory conditions, ITB syndromeBleeding disorders, varicose veins at site, active skin inflammation
Dry NeedlingAcupuncture needles into trigger points — elicit local twitch response; different from acupuncture (anatomical targets, not meridian-based)1–3 min per point, 3–5 pointsTrigger point pain, post-stroke spasticity, DOMSNeedle phobia, anticoagulants (relative), pregnancy (certain regions), pacemaker (e-stim added)
🟡 Manipulative Therapy in BNYS Practice
BNYS practitioners integrate manipulative approaches within naturopathic frameworks. Key principle: manipulation addresses structural obstructions to the body's self-healing force (Vis Medicatrix Naturae). Always combine with: hydrotherapy (contrast baths pre-treatment to warm tissues), nutritional anti-inflammatories (turmeric, omega-3), yoga-based stretching post-treatment, and patient education on posture/ergonomics. Documentation: record technique, segment treated, post-treatment response, and patient consent.

cards.title: Absolute & Relative Contraindications — Manipulation

alerts.label: Relative Contraindications — Proceed with Caution / Modify Technique
alerts.text: Hypermobility (EDS, Marfan), pregnancy (avoid lumbar HVLA T3 onward), inflammatory arthritis in acute flare, anticoagulant therapy, spinal stenosis with neurological symptoms, spondylolisthesis >Grade II.

rows: Palliative soft tissue work only with oncology approval
Defer; gentle mobilisation only with physician clearance

cards._legacyHtml:
⚠️ ABSOLUTE CONTRAINDICATIONS — Do NOT Manipulate
ContraindicationReasonAlternative
Vertebral fracture (acute or suspected)Risk of cord injury, displacementRefer to orthopaedic/emergency
Bone malignancy at target sitePathological fracture riskPalliative soft tissue work only with oncology approval
Active spinal infection (osteomyelitis, discitis)May spread infection, neurological compromiseRefer to spinal specialist
Cervical: acute atlanto-axial instabilityCord compression, death riskRefer neurosurgery
Cauda equina syndromeBladder/bowel dysfunction = surgical emergencyEMERGENCY referral — do not manipulate
Active VBI / vertebrobasilar dissectionStroke risk with cervical manipulationThoracic work only; no cervical
Haemorrhagic stroke <6 monthsRe-bleed riskDefer; gentle mobilisation only with physician clearance
Severe osteoporosis (T-score <−3.5)Fragility fracture risk with HVLAGrade I–II mobilisation only
Relative Contraindications — Proceed with Caution / Modify Technique
Hypermobility (EDS, Marfan), pregnancy (avoid lumbar HVLA T3 onward), inflammatory arthritis in acute flare, anticoagulant therapy, spinal stenosis with neurological symptoms, spondylolisthesis >Grade II.
